evidence_text: The study analyzes 567 Claude Code-generated pull requests across 157 diverse open-source projects.
source_excerpt: “In this paper, we empirically study 567 GitHub pull requests (PRs) generated using Claude Code, an agentic coding tool, across 157 diverse open-source projects.”
note: Study scope is limited to the sampled PR set described in the paper.
caveat: Study scope is limited to the sampled PR set described in the paper.

evidence_text: Agentic PRs were identified by searching GitHub PR descriptions for the string “Generated with Claude Code.”
source_excerpt: “To identify these Agentic-PRs, we searched for those containing the comment string ‘Generated with Claude Code’ in their descriptions.”
note: This identification method captures explicitly labeled PRs rather than all possible Claude Code-assisted PRs.
caveat: This identification method captures explicitly labeled PRs rather than all possible Claude Code-assisted PRs.

evidence_text: The initial Agentic-PR search covered PRs submitted between February 24, 2025 and April 30, 2025 in repositories with at least 10 GitHub stars, yielding 797 PRs before filtering.
source_excerpt: “We conducted this search using the GitHub GraphQL API, focusing on PRs submitted between February 24, 2025 ... and April 30, 2025 ... To ensure relevance and quality, we limited this search to repositories with at least 10 GitHub stars. This search yielded 797 PRs.”
note: The paper treats this as the initial retrieval set before exclusion steps.
caveat: The paper treats this as the initial retrieval set before exclusion steps.

evidence_text: After excluding 54 open PRs, the study retained 743 Agentic-PRs before constructing the matched comparison dataset.
source_excerpt: “We excluded 54 PRs that remained open (i.e., still under development). We identified 743 Agentic-PRs after filtering.”
note: This is an intermediate filtered set, not the final analysis set used for the matched agent-vs-human comparison.
caveat: This is an intermediate filtered set, not the final analysis set used for the matched agent-vs-human comparison.

evidence_text: The human comparison set was matched by author and repository, and 176 Agentic-PRs were excluded because a same-author human comparison set could not be obtained in a similar time frame.
source_excerpt: “Specifically, we selected PRs created during a similar time frame and by the same author, originating from the same project repositories... We excluded 176 Agentic-PRs from the agent-assisted coding dataset because we could not obtain the same number of Human-PRs created during a similar time frame and by the same author.”
note: The final paired dataset reflects this matching constraint rather than the full retrieved Agentic-PR population.
caveat: The final paired dataset reflects this matching constraint rather than the full retrieved Agentic-PR population.

evidence_text: 475 of 567 Agentic-PRs were merged, corresponding to an 83.77425% acceptance rate.
source_excerpt: “Table 2 shows that 475 out of 567 APRs were merged... 83.77425% of Agentic-PRs are accepted by human reviewers.”
note: Acceptance is defined in the study as merged PRs.
caveat: Acceptance is defined in the study as merged PRs.

evidence_text: The acceptance rate for Agentic-PRs was lower than for Human-PRs: 83.77425% versus 91.00529%, and the paper reports this difference as statistically significant.
source_excerpt: “83.77425% of Agentic-PRs are accepted by human reviewers, although their acceptance rate remains statistically lower than Human-PRs (91.00529%). A chi-squared test revealed that Human-PRs have a statistically significantly (p < 0.05) higher acceptance rate than Agentic-PRs.”
note: This evidence introduces a direct comparison showing lower acceptance for Agentic-PRs within the matched dataset.
caveat: This evidence introduces a direct comparison showing lower acceptance for Agentic-PRs within the matched dataset.

evidence_text: Median time to merge was 1.23 hours for Agentic-PRs and 1.04 hours for Human-PRs, with no significant difference reported.
source_excerpt: “A log-rank test shows no significant difference in merge times between Agentic-PRs and Human-PRs... The median time to merge for APRs is 1.23 hours, while HPRs take 1.04 hours.”
note: The source reports similar review timing despite the lower acceptance rate for Agentic-PRs.
caveat: The source reports similar review timing despite the lower acceptance rate for Agentic-PRs.

evidence_text: Among rejected Agentic-PRs, 12.1% were rejected because another PR or developer implemented a different solution first.
source_excerpt: “Table 3 shows that alternative solutions (12.1%)... frequently result in Agentic-PR rejections,” and the table lists “Are implementated by other PRs/developers ... 12.1%.”
note: This is one named rejection category among rejected Agentic-PRs.
caveat: This is one named rejection category among rejected Agentic-PRs.

evidence_text: Among rejected Agentic-PRs, 5.5% were PRs created only to trigger automated verification and were not intended for merging.
source_excerpt: “Process-related issues such as verification-only submissions (5.5%)...” and the table lists “Submission for verification ... 5.5%.”
note: The paper treats these as workflow-related rejections rather than code-quality judgments.
caveat: The paper treats these as workflow-related rejections rather than code-quality judgments.

evidence_text: 63.7% of rejected Agentic-PRs were closed without explanatory comments or discussion.
source_excerpt: “The majority (63.7%) of rejected Agentic-PRs were closed without explanatory comments or discussion. Table 3 shows that 58 out of 91 rejected APRs fell into this category.”
note: This leaves most rejection causes unobserved in the available review record.
caveat: This leaves most rejection causes unobserved in the available review record.

evidence_text: Among merged PRs, 54.94737% of Agentic-PRs and 58.52713% of Human-PRs were merged without any revision, with no statistically significant difference reported.
source_excerpt: “Among merged PRs, we observe that 54.94737% of APRs (261 PRs) are merged as-is with a single commit, as compared to 58.52713% (302 PRs) of HPRs. In addition, the chi-squared test revealed no statistically significant difference between the two groups.”
note: This comparison concerns merged PRs only, not the full submitted set.
caveat: This comparison concerns merged PRs only, not the full submitted set.

evidence_text: When revisions were required, both Agentic-PRs and Human-PRs typically had a median of 2 revised commits before merge.
source_excerpt: “Analysis of revision patterns shows that both APRs and HPRs typically include a median of 2 revised commits prior to merge.”
note: The paper reports this as part of the revision-effort comparison.
caveat: The paper reports this as part of the revision-effort comparison.

evidence_text: Refactoring was more common in Agentic-PRs than Human-PRs, at 24.9% versus 14.9% in the manually classified PR sample.
source_excerpt: “Code refactoring is more frequent in Agentic-PRs than Human-PRs, accounting for 24.9% of Agentic-PRs compared to 14.9% of Human-PRs.”
note: These percentages come from the manually classified RQ1 sample.
caveat: These percentages come from the manually classified RQ1 sample.

evidence_text: Test-related changes were more common in Agentic-PRs than Human-PRs, at 18.8% versus 4.5% in the manually classified PR sample.
source_excerpt: “Test-related improvements are substantially more common in Agentic-PRs than in Human-PRs (18.8% versus 4.5%).”
note: These percentages are reported from the RQ1 classification sample.
caveat: These percentages are reported from the RQ1 classification sample.

evidence_text: Among revised Agentic-PRs, the most common revision categories were bug fixes (45.1%), documentation updates (27.4%), refactoring (25.7%), and style changes (22.1%).
source_excerpt: “In this paper, we empirically study 567 GitHub pull requests (PRs) generated using Claude Code, an agentic coding tool, across 157 diverse open-source projects.”
note: Study scope is limited to the sampled PR set described in the paper.
caveat: Study scope is limited to the sampled PR set described in the paper.
